## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about the 20x rule of Evergreen. There is a huge mistake that almost everybody makes when they try to take their webinar Evergreen. And that is, they try to go Evergreen far too early. Here's the 20 times rule of Evergreen. Don't go Evergreen until you've presented your webinar live at least 20 times. What? Why? Because you should be getting better all the time. You'll learn lessons every time you present it, both good and bad lessons. And so here's my recommendation. Record every webinar that you present. You and your team should take notes immediately after each presentation while it's fresh on your minds. And it probably should be in the format of something like this. What we call WWDD. Wintwell do differently. So this is what we do. This is what went well. This is what we should do differently in future broadcasts. And then every time five times you give your webinar, do a careful study of each of them. And then making changes to your script, reflecting the key learnings. Now you should make those changes consistently throughout that process. But at least every five times you should sit down and do a meta review of all of the last five. And make sure you're really incorporating everything. This is not only the script, but it's your tone of voice as you say a certain line. Not just your tone of voice all the way through, but your tone of voice at this certain line, the pause at this particular point. You're looking at, you're refining the process down to a micro performance level. This is what makes Broadway work. You know, attending Broadway on opening night is a really big deal. You get a better performance if you attend Broadway a month to two months later. They've been through another 20, 30 performances by then. That's the time to watch it because now we're a peak performance level and they're not yet bored. So after 15 performances, take their top five converting performances throughout all the 15 ones you've done so far. Steady them in detail and have your team steady them in detail and have an outsider steady your top three. Sometimes you're going to have to pay money to have that happen. That's fine. And then implement the resulting changes. Then after 20 performances, you should be seeing consistent conversion rates. So your last three to five, your conversion rate should be pretty flat lined by that time. You should be pretty good at that time. If they're not consistent, you probably should do more until you see consistency happening. Then at that point, you choose your best performance and make that the control.

## [03:04 - 06:04]

Your webinar recording, the one that you're going to use throughout your webinars going from that point on. And so that becomes the webinar recording. Now, optionally, do an edit from your top performances, combining the best performances, best pieces from the best performances into one recording. So you have like the best of the best. Now the challenge, if you do that, the webinar tools that basically enable you to carry chat over in your recording. And so people are seeing chat as if it's happening live. You won't be able to have that if you do what I just said, because you're actually putting in a different recording in there. But this isn't a huge problem because you're going to get that stream rebuilt fairly quickly and then make sure you test this against your best recording to prove that it's actually better. So I'm going to tell you a quick story here. When I worked for Kimberly Clark, the Kleenex brand was working on trying to reduce the cost of the product that they were putting out. And so they here was the base Kleenex. And so then they made this slight tweak, which would save, you know, X hundred thousand dollars a year or something like that. And they ran that. And so then they tested the control, the base case versus the new, let's call it test number one, they ran that test and then the test actually performed at least as well as the control did. And it's like, great, we just saved two hundred thousand dollars X per year. That's wonderful. So then they took and they ran another, they made another change and they tested that against test one, the new winner. Okay. And then they did that until he got a winner that that was at least equal to or if not better than test one. So that's called that's called test two. Then they ran test three and they did the same thing testing against tests two. And after they had done that like five times down the thing and had a couple of million dollars in annual savings, I don't know how many million, a bunch of million. And then they finally took, let's call that test five and they tested test five against test one. And what they found was it performed miserably against test one. So you always need to make sure you test against a control. And so when you go out and you run your optimized recording with all the best parts from all the different webinars, do make sure you test that against your best recording to make sure that it really is better. Because there may be decent things that you don't realize were happening in there that just aren't there anymore.

## [06:04 - 07:30]

Now, when I teach this to people, I consistently hear them saying the same thing, but Don, I'm going to get bored doing the same webinar over and over again. Can't I just quit earlier than that? I view this process exactly the opposite way. I view it that every webinar performance is is a game, it's a contest to beat all the others. And I ask myself, what can I do to achieve that goal? And so I'm coming in saying this is going to be my best performance ever. And I'm psyched and I'm ready to go to do that. I've done everything I can to achieve that goal. And I'm going to try to beat it. And so I never get bored doing this kind of stuff. I'll do it over and over again, because the thing I'm always trying to do is I'm trying to beat the best bonus tip. Don't break the 20 x rule of webinars. It really is worth it to do it this way. I know it's hard. But it's worth it because this is the kind of thing that can literally create a constant stream of revenue that can go on with a little bit of work to constantly tweak it, go on for months and maybe even well beyond that.
